title: Borrowing lecture notes
transcript: Hey, I missed yesterday's lecture. Do you think I could borrow your notes to copy them?

options: Yeah, the lecture was pretty dull, honestly.
I didn't miss any classes this week.
Sure, no problem — I'll send you a photo of them tonight.
I prefer taking my notes on a laptop.
questions.explanation: The speaker is politely asking to borrow notes, so agreeing and offering to share them (option 2) is the natural, cooperative response. Option 0 comments on the lecture instead of answering the request; option 1 talks about the responder's own attendance and ignores the favor; option 3 is a self-focused remark that never addresses whether the notes can be shared.

title: Professor offers an extension
transcript: If you're having trouble finishing the essay, I can give you a couple of extra days.

options: That would really help — thank you. I'll have it in by Friday.
The library closes at ten on weekdays.
I already finished reading the assigned chapters.
No, I didn't think the essay was too long.
questions.explanation: The professor is offering more time, so gratefully accepting and naming a new deadline (option 0) fits perfectly. Option 1 is unrelated information about the library; option 2 answers a question that wasn't asked; option 3 misreads the offer as a comment about the essay's length.

title: Roommate asks to lower the music
transcript: Would you mind turning the music down a little? I've got an exam first thing tomorrow.

options: Your exam is in the science building, isn't it?
I just bought these speakers last week.
Music actually helps me focus.
Oh, sorry — I didn't realize. I'll put my headphones on.
questions.explanation: A polite request to lower the volume calls for an apology and a fix (option 3). Option 0 redirects to the exam's location and ignores the request; option 1 changes the subject to the speakers; option 2 is dismissive and refuses to accommodate the roommate.

title: Directions to the registrar
transcript: Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the registrar's office from here?

options: I registered for four classes this semester.
Sure — it's on the second floor of the admin building, just past the elevators.
You'll need to make an appointment first.
The staff there were really helpful when I called.
questions.explanation: The person wants directions, so giving the location and route (option 1) is the appropriate reply. Option 0 plays on the word 'register' but gives no directions; option 2 adds a requirement without saying how to get there; option 3 comments on the staff rather than the way to the office.

title: Scheduling a group meeting
transcript: Are you free to meet up this weekend to work on the group presentation?

options: The presentation went really well, thanks.
I think our topic is climate policy.
Saturday afternoon works for me — how about the library at two?
I usually don't enjoy working in groups.
questions.explanation: The question asks about availability, so proposing a specific time and place (option 2) is the natural response. Option 0 uses the past tense as if the presentation already happened; option 1 names the topic instead of answering about meeting; option 3 is a dismissive remark that avoids committing.

title: Ordering coffee
transcript: Would you like me to leave room for cream, or fill it all the way up?

options: Leave a little room, please — I take mine with cream.
Just a small coffee, thanks.
I'll pay with my card.
Do you have any muffins left?
questions.explanation: The barista is asking a yes-or-no style choice about cream, so stating the preference (option 0) answers it directly. Option 1 answers an earlier question about size; option 2 jumps ahead to payment; option 3 asks an unrelated new question.

title: Feedback after a presentation
transcript: You made some strong points today, though you did run a bit over the time limit.

options: No, I don't think anyone else presented today.
The projector kept freezing on me.
I still haven't picked a topic.
Thank you — I'll try to keep it tighter next time.
questions.explanation: The comment mixes praise with gentle criticism, so accepting both graciously (option 3) is ideal. Option 0 answers a question that wasn't asked; option 1 makes an excuse and ignores the compliment; option 2 refers to a topic as if the presentation hadn't happened yet.

title: Offer to help move
transcript: I heard you're moving into a new apartment on Saturday. Do you need a hand?

options: The new place has two bedrooms.
That's really kind of you — an extra pair of hands would be great.
I moved here from Chicago last year.
The gym gets pretty crowded on Saturdays.
questions.explanation: The friend is offering help, so thanking them and accepting (option 1) is the warm, natural reply. Option 0 shares a detail about the apartment but ignores the offer; option 2 talks about a past move; option 3 changes the subject to the gym.

title: Advisor questions the course load
transcript: Taking five classes while working part-time might be a lot. Are you sure you can manage it?

options: All my classes are in the morning.
I work at the campus bookstore.
I've thought it over, and I think I can handle it if I stay organized.
Five has always been my lucky number.
questions.explanation: The advisor is voicing concern about workload, so reassuring them with a reasoned answer (option 2) addresses it directly. Option 0 gives a schedule detail that doesn't settle the concern; option 1 mentions the job without addressing whether it's manageable; option 3 is an absurd, off-tone reply.

title: A found umbrella
transcript: Is this your umbrella? I found it by the door on my way in.

options: They say it'll rain all week.
I usually take the bus when it's raining.
You can leave lost items at the front desk.
Oh, it is mine — thank you so much for grabbing it!
questions.explanation: The speaker is checking whether the umbrella belongs to the listener, so confirming and thanking them (option 3) fits. Option 0 comments on the weather; option 1 talks about commuting; option 2 gives lost-and-found advice as if the umbrella weren't the listener's own.

title: Dinner invitation
transcript: We're all heading to the dining hall for dinner around six. Want to come along?

options: Sure, count me in — I'll meet you at the entrance.
I had a sandwich for lunch.
The dining hall added a new salad bar.
I already had breakfast, thanks.
questions.explanation: This is an invitation to dinner, so accepting and arranging where to meet (option 0) is the natural response. Option 1 refers to an earlier meal; option 2 shares trivia about the dining hall instead of replying to the invite; option 3 declines by referencing breakfast, which makes no sense for a dinner plan.

title: Library checkout
transcript: This book is due back in two weeks, but you can renew it online if you need more time.

options: I'd like to buy this one, please.
Great, thanks — I'll renew it if I haven't finished by then.
This book was written back in the 1990s.
Is the café upstairs still open?
questions.explanation: The librarian is explaining the due date and the renewal option, so acknowledging it and planning to renew (option 1) is the fitting reply. Option 0 misunderstands a library loan as a purchase; option 2 comments on when the book was written; option 3 asks an unrelated question about the café.

title: Card declined at the café
transcript: I'm sorry, but your card was just declined. Do you have another way to pay?

options: The coffee here is so much better than at the place across the street.
Oh, that's odd — let me try a different card.
Yes, I'd like to order a large latte, please.
I usually stop by here on my way to class.
questions.explanation: The cashier says the card didn't go through and asks for another payment method, so offering a different card (option 1) directly addresses the problem. Option 0 comments on the coffee's quality; option 2 places an order as if payment hadn't come up; option 3 makes small talk about the responder's routine instead of solving the payment issue.

title: Congratulations on an internship
transcript: I heard you landed the summer internship at the museum — that's fantastic news!

options: The museum is closed on Mondays, I'm pretty sure.
No, I haven't been to the museum in years.
Thanks so much! I still can't quite believe it.
You should apply there too — it's a great place.
questions.explanation: This is warm congratulations, so a grateful, pleased reply (option 2) fits naturally. Option 0 offers unrelated trivia about hours; option 1 misreads the news as a question about visiting; option 3 turns the attention to the other person and never acknowledges the congratulations.

title: Is this seat free?
transcript: Excuse me, is anyone sitting here, or is this seat free?

options: I've been studying in here since this morning.
The library stays open until midnight tonight.
I usually like the desks over by the window.
Go ahead, it's all yours — nobody's using it.
questions.explanation: The question asks whether a seat is taken, so confirming it's free (option 3) is the direct, natural answer. Option 0 talks about how long the responder has been there; option 1 gives the library's hours; option 2 shares a seating preference — none of them actually tell the person whether they can sit down.

title: Invited to office hours
transcript: If the problem set is giving you trouble, feel free to stop by my office hours on Thursday.

options: Thanks — I'll come by Thursday afternoon; I do have a few questions.
Those problems weren't on the exam last year.
I already turned in the reading response.
Your lectures are on Tuesdays and Thursdays, right?
questions.explanation: The professor offers help during office hours, so accepting and naming a time (option 0) is the appropriate reply. Option 1 comments on old exams; option 2 refers to a different assignment; option 3 asks about the lecture schedule instead of taking up the offer of help.

title: Ordering a drink first
transcript: Can I start you off with something to drink while you look over the menu?

options: The table by the window was already taken when we came in.
Could I get the check whenever you're ready?
Yes, I'll have an iced tea, please.
This place gets really crowded on weekends.
questions.explanation: The server is offering to take a drink order, so naming a drink (option 2) is the natural response. Option 0 remarks on the seating; option 1 asks for the bill before the meal has even started, which doesn't fit; option 3 makes an off-topic comment about how busy the restaurant gets.

title: Borrowing a phone charger
transcript: My phone's about to die — do you happen to have a charger I could borrow?

options: I picked up a new phone case last week.
Sure, here you go — just give it back before you head out.
My battery usually lasts me the whole day.
There's a great phone store just downtown.
questions.explanation: The speaker is asking to borrow a charger, so lending one with a small condition (option 1) is the cooperative, fitting reply. Option 0 changes the subject to a phone case; option 2 talks about the responder's own battery instead of helping; option 3 suggests a store rather than answering the immediate request.

title: Rain during the picnic
transcript: It looks like it might rain this afternoon. Should we move the picnic inside?

options: I packed some sandwiches and a bit of fruit.
The park has really nice walking trails.
I've never been much good at predicting the weather.
Good idea — let's set up in the common room instead.
questions.explanation: The speaker suggests relocating the picnic because of rain, so agreeing and proposing an indoor spot (option 3) answers the question directly. Option 0 mentions the food; option 1 praises the park; option 2 sidesteps the decision with a joke about forecasting, none of which resolves where to hold the picnic.

title: Returning a jacket
transcript: Was there something wrong with the jacket, or would you just like to exchange it for a different size?

options: The size was just a little too small — I'd like a medium instead.
I bought a pair of shoes here last month too.
This jacket comes in three different colors.
Your store stays open pretty late on Fridays.
questions.explanation: The clerk asks whether the jacket was defective or the customer wants a different size, so explaining the fit and requesting another size (option 0) responds directly. Option 1 brings up an unrelated past purchase; option 2 states a product detail; option 3 comments on store hours — none of them answer the clerk's question.
